title: Refactor beads hooks into shared core module for maintainability
description:
Background
Current beads hooks are separate entrypoints with partial shared utilities in hooks/beads-gate-utils.mjs. As behavior evolves, policy and messaging drift risk increases.

Goal
Keep separate hook entrypoints for Claude wiring, but centralize shared decision logic/messages into reusable modules.

Implementation checklist (ordered)
1) Baseline + branch
- Create feature branch from main
- Run: npm test --prefix cli -- test/hooks.test.ts
- Record baseline pass/fail in issue notes

2) Create shared core module
- Add hooks/beads-gate-core.mjs
- Export:
  - readHookInputOrExit0()
  - resolveSessionContext(input) -> { cwd, sessionId, beadsProject }
  - resolveClaimAndWorkState(ctx) using beads-gate-utils adapters
  - decideEditGate(state)
  - decideCommitGate(state, cmd)
  - decideStopGate(state)
  - prepareCloseAction(state)
- Keep module pure where possible (return decisions; wrapper performs process.exit/write)

3) Create shared message templates
- Add hooks/beads-gate-messages.mjs
- Centralize all repeated user-facing strings currently duplicated in entrypoints
- Include template functions for edit/commit/stop block messages

4) Refactor entrypoint hooks into thin wrappers
- hooks/beads-edit-gate.mjs
- hooks/beads-commit-gate.mjs
- hooks/beads-stop-gate.mjs
- hooks/beads-close-memory-prompt.mjs
Rules:
- keep current hook filenames unchanged
- no settings matcher changes
- wrappers should parse input, call core, emit message/output, exit 0/2

5) Preserve adapter boundary
- Keep hooks/beads-gate-utils.mjs as low-level bd/shell adapter layer
- Do not move external command execution into entrypoints
- Use existing fail-open strategy for unexpected errors

6) Tests
- Extend cli/test/hooks.test.ts with decision-matrix coverage:
  - session has claim -> allow edit/commit/stop behavior as expected
  - no claim + open-only work -> edit blocks
  - no claim + no work -> edit allows
  - no session_id fallback path
  - bd unavailable -> fail-open
- Keep wrapper smoke tests (imports + exit behavior)

7) Docs
- Update hooks/README.md with architecture section:
  - entrypoints vs core vs utils vs messages
  - where policy changes should be made
  - where message changes should be made

8) Verification + scope check
- Run: npm test --prefix cli -- test/hooks.test.ts
- Optionally run full: npm test --prefix cli
- Confirm changed files are expected only

Validation commands
- npm test --prefix cli -- test/hooks.test.ts
- rg -n "beads-gate-core|beads-gate-messages" hooks cli/test/hooks.test.ts
- git diff --name-only

Expected files touched
- hooks/beads-gate-core.mjs (new)
- hooks/beads-gate-messages.mjs (new)
- hooks/beads-edit-gate.mjs
- hooks/beads-commit-gate.mjs
- hooks/beads-stop-gate.mjs
- hooks/beads-close-memory-prompt.mjs
- hooks/README.md
- cli/test/hooks.test.ts
- (optional) hooks/beads-gate-utils.mjs if adapter API needs extension

Non-goals
- No hook matcher/wiring changes in settings
- No behavior expansion unless needed to preserve current contract

Acceptance criteria
- Shared core + messages modules exist and are used by all beads hook entrypoints
- Hook files are thin wrappers with no duplicated policy logic
- Existing behavior preserved (or intentionally documented in PR notes)
- Tests cover matrix and pass in CI
- README documents module boundaries and edit locations

Risks / constraints
- Hooks run independently/parallel; avoid cross-hook state coupling
- Preserve strict hook exit semantics: 0 allow, 2 block
- Keep fail-open for unexpected runtime failures

Effort estimate
- Engineering: 3-5 hours
- Review + polish: 1-2 hours
- Total: ~0.5 to 1 day

discovered-from:xtrm-xmy
close_reason: Completed: Created beads-gate-core.mjs and beads-gate-messages.mjs, refactored all 4 entrypoint hooks into thin wrappers (~35 lines each), added 10 new tests for core functions, updated README with architecture docs. All 40 tests pass.

title: Tests: comprehensive xtrm CLI test suite covering all commands
description:
## Context
The xtrm CLI has no comprehensive test coverage of its command surface. As the install commands are restructured (v8h), beads dep check added (zu7), and help improved (n5s), tests must be co-developed or written immediately after to prevent regressions.

## Scope
Test all xtrm commands:

### xtrm install all
- Dry run: verify manifest output, no file writes
- With mocked beads+dolt present: installs beads hooks
- With mocked beads+dolt absent + --yes: skips beads hooks, no error
- With mocked beads+dolt absent + interactive: prompts correctly
- Files land in correct locations (~/.claude/hooks/, ~/.claude/skills/, mcp config)

### xtrm install basic
- Installs skills + general hooks + mcp
- Does NOT install beads hooks
- No beads/dolt dependency check performed

### xtrm install project
- Installs project-skills into test project dir
- settings.json deep merge is non-destructive (does not clobber existing hooks)
- Fails with clear message outside git repo

### xtrm help / xtrm (no args)
- Banner present in output
- All component sections present (hooks, skills, project-skills, mcp)
- Descriptions sourced from SKILL.md frontmatter

### Flags
- --dry-run: zero writes, exit 0
- --yes: no prompts, correct defaults
- --quiet: no banner
- --force: overwrites existing files

## Test infrastructure
- Use existing test runner (npm test)
- Mock filesystem writes (do not actually write to ~/.claude/ in tests)
- Mock external commands: bd, dolt, specialists
- Snapshot tests for help output to catch regressions in descriptions

## Acceptance criteria
- npm test passes with new tests
- Coverage: all install subcommands + flags tested
- No test writes to real ~/.claude/ directory
- CI passes (GitHub Actions workflow)

## Open idea review
Should there be integration tests that actually install into a temp HOME directory (using a temp /home/dawid env override) to catch real path-resolution bugs? Unit tests with mocks are faster but can miss environmental issues. Consider a two-tier approach: unit tests for logic, one integration smoke test per install profile against a temp HOME.
close_reason: Done: CLI test suite implemented

title: CLI: reduce interactivity, add --yes / --dry-run, enforce banner on all views
description:
## Context
The current xtrm CLI is heavily interactive (prompts at each step). For automation, CI, and power users this is friction. The goal is sensible defaults + opt-in interactivity, not zero-interaction. The banner (ASCII art / branding) must appear consistently on all views that involve the xtrm command.

## Changes

### Reduce interactivity
- Every prompt that has a safe default should default to that default on Enter (no input)
- Add --yes / -y flag globally: accepts all prompts (for scripting/CI)
- Add --no-interactive flag: same as --yes but explicitly signals automation mode
- Prompts should only appear when a decision is genuinely consequential (e.g. 'overwrite existing file?', 'install beads+dolt?')
- Remove prompts for things that are always the right action (e.g. 'proceed with install? y/n' when user already typed xtrm install all)

### Subcommand/flag improvements
- Each install profile (all/basic/project) is a positional subcommand arg, not a prompted choice
- Add --component flag for fine-grained installs: xtrm install --component=hooks, xtrm install --component=skills
- Ensure clear error messages when run from wrong context (e.g. xtrm install project outside a git repo)

### Banner
- Banner (ASCII art xtrm branding) appears at top of: xtrm, xtrm help, xtrm install *, xtrm version
- Banner is suppressed with --no-banner or --quiet flag for scripting
- Banner includes version number

## Acceptance criteria
- xtrm install all --yes runs to completion without any prompts (except beads install which requires explicit consent — see zu7)
- xtrm install all --dry-run prints full manifest, no writes, exits 0
- Banner appears in all major command views
- --quiet suppresses banner for scripting
- No regression on existing interactive behaviour

## Open idea review
Should the CLI ship with a config file (~/.xtrm.json or ~/.config/xtrm/config.json) where users can set their defaults once (e.g. always --yes, preferred install profile)? This would eliminate repetitive flags for power users but adds a config surface to maintain.
close_reason: Closed

title: CLI: xtrm help — comprehensive package overview and component catalogue
description:
## Context
xtrm help (and xtrm with no args) should give a complete, readable overview of everything in the package. Currently the help is minimal CLI flag output. Users should be able to run xtrm help and understand the full scope of what gets installed and why.

## Structure
Display banner first (always), then:

### Install commands
Brief description of each install profile (all / basic / project) and what they do.

### Global hooks
List each hook in hooks/ with:
- File name
- Event it fires on (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, etc.)
- One-line description of what it does
Example:
  main-guard.mjs        PreToolUse      Blocks edits and commits on main/master
  beads-edit-gate.mjs   PreToolUse      Blocks file edits if no issue is claimed
  beads-stop-gate.mjs   Stop            Blocks session stop with unclosed claims

### Skills
List each skill in skills/ with its one-line description from SKILL.md frontmatter.

### Project skills
List each skill in project-skills/ with one-line description and what it installs.

### MCP servers
List any MCP servers registered by xtrm, with brief description.

## Implementation notes
- Source descriptions dynamically from SKILL.md YAML frontmatter where available
- Fall back to hook file header comment for hook descriptions
- xtrm (no args) should show this overview + 'Run xtrm install all to get started'
- Banner must appear at the top of every view

## Acceptance criteria
- xtrm help shows all installed components grouped by type
- xtrm (no args) shows overview + getting-started prompt
- All descriptions are accurate and sourced from SKILL.md frontmatter or hook headers
- Output is readable in a standard terminal (80-col friendly)

## Open idea review
Should xtrm help --installed show only what is currently installed on this machine (diff against ~/.claude/), giving users a status view vs the full catalogue? This would be valuable for auditing and debugging but adds complexity. Consider whether a xtrm status command is a better home for this.
close_reason: Implemented on feat/zu7-n5s

title: main-guard: default-deny Bash with safe allowlist on protected branches
description:
## Context
Issue xyi identified that arbitrary Bash file-write commands (cat >, tee, cp, mv, sed -i) bypass main-guard on protected branches. Reviewer comment #4 on xyi recommends the stronger fix: default-deny all Bash on protected branches, with a narrow allowlist of safe/non-mutating commands.

## Reviewer recommendation (xyi comment #4)
'On protected branches, default-deny Bash with a small allowlist for safe/non-mutating branch-exit and inspection commands, plus an explicit emergency override path.'

## Proposed allowlist (safe on protected branches)
- git checkout -b <name>     (create feature branch — the intended exit path)
- git switch -c <name>       (same)
- git status / git log / git diff / git branch (read-only inspection)
- git stash                  (safe, doesn't write to branch)
- bd <any>                   (beads issue tracking, no file mutations)
- cat <file> (read-only, no redirect)

## Block by default
- Any command containing output redirect: >, >>
- cat/tee/cp/mv/sed -i/awk writing to paths under input.cwd
- git commit / git push (already handled, confirm preserved)
- npm/python/sh script execution (potential indirect writes)

## Emergency override
- Env var: MAIN_GUARD_ALLOW_BASH=1 (escape hatch, visible and explicit)

## Acceptance criteria
- On main, cat > file is blocked
- On main, git status is allowed
- On main, git checkout -b feature/x is allowed
- On main, bd create is allowed
- MAIN_GUARD_ALLOW_BASH=1 bypasses the Bash default-deny
- Existing Write/Edit blocking unchanged

## Open idea review
The allowlist approach assumes we can enumerate safe commands — but shell is composable and creative bypasses exist. Is default-deny Bash the right tradeoff between security and usability? Should there be a 'warn but allow' mode vs hard block, configurable per-project? Consider how often legitimate Bash on main is needed (hot fixes, deploy scripts) and whether that warrants a softer default.
close_reason: Completed

title: Fix beads-gate-utils: replace fragile regex parsing with Total line parsing
description: Reviewer finding (320 comment): getInProgress and getTotalWork count the literal 'in_progress' token, which accidentally matches the status legend line rather than actual issues. --json is not supported. Fix: parse the 'Total: N issues (X open, Y in progress)' summary line from a single bd list call instead.
close_reason: Fixed: Total line parsing replaces fragile regex; T6/T9 fallback paths now verified

title: main-guard: Bash file-write commands bypass protection on main branch
description:
## Bug

The main-guard hook (`~/.claude/hooks/specialists-main-guard.mjs`) protects Write/Edit/MultiEdit/NotebookEdit tools completely, but its Bash protection is narrowly scoped to git operations only.

## Current Bash check

```js
if (tool === 'Bash') {
  if (/^git commit/.test(cmd)) { /* block */ }
  if (/^git push/.test(cmd))   { /* block */ }
  // everything else → process.exit(0)
}
```

## The gap

All shell-based file writes bypass the guard entirely on main:
- `cat > file << 'EOF'`
- `echo 'content' > file`
- `tee file`
- `cp src dst`
- `mv src dst`
- `sed -i`
- `awk '...' > file`

## Observed in this session

All four beads hooks (`beads-edit-gate.mjs`, `beads-stop-gate.mjs`, `beads-commit-gate.mjs`, `beads-close-memory-prompt.mjs`) and the new `beads-gate-utils.mjs` were written via `cat > heredoc` in Bash while on `main`. Main-guard never fired.

Note: those writes targeted `~/.claude/hooks/` (outside the project repo), so they wouldn't have been harmful — but the gap exists for in-repo writes too.

## Proposed fix

**Option A — Pattern matching**: detect common shell file-write patterns in the Bash command string. Brittle — easy to bypass or over-match.

**Option B — Scope check (preferred)**: check whether the Bash command touches files inside `input.cwd` (the project directory). If the command contains a path that falls under the project root, block it on main.

Challenges with Option B:
- Bash commands don't always make target paths explicit or easy to parse
- Multi-command strings (`cmd1 && cmd2`) need full parsing or heuristic scanning
- Could still be bypassed with indirect writes (subshells, scripts)

**Option C — Hybrid**: block known write-pattern prefixes (`cat >`, `tee `, `cp `, `mv `, `echo >`) only when the target path is inside `input.cwd`. Narrow enough to avoid false positives, covers the most common cases.

## Question for reviewer

Is Option C the right tradeoff? Or is this gap acceptable given that the main-guard's primary surface is structured tools (Write/Edit), and Bash is already a power-user escape hatch that implies intentionality?
close_reason: Fixed by main-guard v2 (ea36a92). Default-deny Bash with SAFE_BASH_PATTERNS allowlist is implemented in hooks/main-guard.mjs. All unlisted Bash commands are blocked on protected branches.

This issue is correctly scoped to the global specialists hook:
- Target: `~/.claude/hooks/specialists-main-guard.mjs`
- Not target: `project-skills/main-guard/.claude/hooks/main-guard.cjs`

Reason:
- `project-skills/main-guard` is wired only for `Write|Edit|MultiEdit` via its local `.claude/settings.json` and does not include Bash matching by default.
- The observed bypass came from Bash behavior in `specialists-main-guard.mjs`.

Recommendation:
- Option C (pattern matching known write commands) is acceptable as a short-term mitigation, but it remains bypassable and should not be treated as complete enforcement.
- Preferred long-term fix in `specialists-main-guard.mjs`: on protected branches, default-deny Bash with a small allowlist for safe/non-mutating branch-exit and inspection commands (e.g., `git checkout -b`, `git switch -c`, `git status`, `git branch --show-current`), plus an explicit emergency override path if needed.

Net: keep this as a specialists-global bug. If desired, open a separate enhancement issue for project-skill main-guard to add optional Bash policy coverage.

title: Refactor beads-edit-gate to use session-scoped issue claiming via bd kv
description:
## Problem

The current beads-edit-gate.mjs checks if ANY issue is in_progress globally. In multi-agent or multi-session scenarios (parallel subagents, concurrent sessions), agent B can free-ride on agent A's in_progress issue — making edits without ever claiming work itself. The gate's intent (enforce ownership before editing) is not met in these cases.

## Goal

Gate on whether THIS agent/session has explicitly claimed an issue, not whether anyone has one in_progress.

## Design

### Primitives available
- Hook input JSON includes `session_id` (unique per Claude Code session)
- `bd kv set/get/clear` is project-scoped (stored in .beads/dolt) — confirmed real
- `bd kv list` shows all keys; memories use the `memory.*` prefix

### Claim registry pattern

**Claiming** (agent takes ownership):
```bash
bd update <id> --status=in_progress
bd kv set "claimed:<session_id>" "<issue_id>"
```

**Gate check** (beads-edit-gate.mjs):
```js
const sessionId = input.session_id;
const claimed = execSync(`bd kv get "claimed:${sessionId}"`, { cwd, encoding: 'utf8', ... });
if (!claimed.trim()) {
  // block — this session has not claimed an issue
}
```

**Release** (on close — beads-close-memory-prompt.mjs already fires on bd close):
- Parse closed issue ID from the Bash command
- Run `bd kv clear "claimed:<session_id>"`

### Files to modify
1. `~/.claude/hooks/beads-edit-gate.mjs` (and project-skills source if one exists)
   - Replace global in_progress check with `bd kv get "claimed:<session_id>"` check
   - Fall back to old behavior if session_id is absent (non-Claude environments)
   - Keep the open=0 bypass for clean-start state (no issues at all)

2. `~/.claude/hooks/beads-close-memory-prompt.mjs`
   - After detecting `bd close`, also run `bd kv clear "claimed:<session_id>"`
   - Use session_id from hook input

3. Update SKILL.md for using-main-guard / beads workflow docs to show:
   `bd update <id> --status=in_progress && bd kv set "claimed:<session_id>" "<id>"`
   OR expose a wrapper alias/command

### Open questions before implementing
1. Should we expose a `bd-claim <id>` thin shell wrapper (in hooks/scripts/) to bundle update + kv set in one command, so the workflow stays ergonomic?
2. What should happen if the agent crashes mid-session and the kv claim is never cleared? Consider a TTL or session-start cleanup of stale claim keys.
3. beads-stop-gate.mjs and beads-commit-gate.mjs also use global in_progress — should they be updated in the same pass to check session-scoped claims instead?

## Acceptance criteria
- Agent A has issue X in_progress; agent B cannot edit files without running its own claim
- Single-agent workflow unchanged: claim → edit → close → claim cleared automatically
- Gate fails open (exit 0) if bd kv is unavailable or session_id is missing
- Tests cover: claimed session allowed, unclaimed session blocked, multi-session isolation
close_reason: Implemented session-scoped claiming in all four beads hooks via bd kv

title: beads-edit-gate fires on legitimate close-then-write workflow
description:
The beads-edit-gate blocks writes immediately after closing completed issues, even when the agent is still in the middle of a session with follow-up work. Two friction patterns observed:

1. Close-then-write: batch-closing multiple done issues (bd close A B C) leaves in_progress=0, then the next Write/Edit fires the gate before a new issue is created. Forces redundant re-marking cycle every time work transitions.

2. Post-compaction reset: after /compact, all in_progress issues revert to open in the gate's view — the compaction hook (bd prime) injects context but doesn't restore status. Agent must re-run bd update --status=in_progress before the first write of the resumed session.

Root cause: gate checks live bd list --status=in_progress with a hard 0=block rule. No grace period, no awareness of recent close, no session-level tracking concept.

Possible fixes to evaluate:
- Allow if ALL issues are closed and none are open (session-complete state is valid)
- Grace period: if bd close ran in the last N seconds, skip the check
- Session issue: auto-create/suggest a session tracking issue at start via SessionStart hook
- Gate tolerance: skip for markdown/docs-only files when no in_progress exists
close_reason: Closed

title: feat(xt-init): Project Bootstrap should seed .xtrm/registry.json in target repo
description: PROBLEM: xt init's Project Bootstrap phase scaffolds .xtrm/{config,hooks,skills} into the target repo but does NOT create .xtrm/registry.json — the file 'xt update --root' uses to detect managed repos. Consequence: a freshly xt-init'd repo shows as 'incomplete' in 'xt update --root' sweeps until the operator manually copies/seeds registry.json from xtrm-tools (or runs an undocumented workflow). Confirmed 2026-05-12 with mercury/darth-feedor and mercury/terminalbeta — both required cp of /home/dawid/dev/xtrm-tools/.xtrm/registry.json after xt init before they showed as 'refreshed'. SUCCESS: 'xt init' (or a sub-phase) writes a snapshot of the current package's .xtrm/registry.json into the target repo's .xtrm/. After init, 'xt update --root' classifies the repo as 'managed' (not 'incomplete'). SCOPE: cli/src/commands/init.ts Project Bootstrap phase or its install-runtime call; cli/src/core/install logic — wherever the registry copy currently SHOULD be happening but isn't. NON_GOALS: do not change registry format; do not change xt update detection logic. CONSTRAINTS: registry snapshot should match the package version that produced it (so drift detection can later flag if the consumer is far behind). VALIDATION: xt init in a fresh repo → .xtrm/registry.json exists with content matching package source. OUTPUT: code + regression test in cli/test/init-cli.test.ts or cli/test/init-phases.test.ts. Post-release work — workaround documented at memory xt-init-s-project-bootstrap-phase-does-not.

title: fix(pi-runtime): npm-installed Pi packages reported as installedVersion: null by xt update
description: PROBLEM: Pi packages globally installed via npm (@jaggerxtrm/pi-extensions@0.7.17, @aliou/pi-guardrails, @aliou/pi-processes, @robhowley/pi-structured-return, @zenobius/pi-worktrees, pi-coding-agent, gitnexus etc.) are correctly listed under 'npm ls -g --depth=0', but 'xt update' reports each as 'state: missing, installedVersion: null'. Listing in the output is therefore misleading — operators believe packages need install when they're already present. REPRO: with packages installed globally, run 'xt update --json' and observe each pi package returns installedVersion: null. Likely cause: detection layer reads from an expected install path that no longer matches where npm puts packages, OR npm scope mismatch (e.g. detection uses bare name 'pi-worktrees' but actual install is '@zenobius/pi-worktrees'). SCOPE: cli/src/core/pi-runtime.ts (the freshness/version-detection path for piPackages), cli/src/tests/pi-runtime-safeguards.test.ts. NON_GOALS: do not change xt-managed package list. VALIDATION: with all 8 packages installed globally, 'xt update --json' returns 'state: current' (or 'outdated' if applicable) for each — never 'missing'. OUTPUT: detection-layer fix + regression test. Post-release work — does not block 0.7.18 since the packages ARE installed; only the display is wrong.

SUCCESS: market-data xt update --apply succeeds; darth-feedor + terminalbeta have valid registry after xt init. SCOPE: /home/dawid/projects/mercury/market-data/.xtrm/skills/user/packs/market-data/PACK.json (1-line edit, cross-repo); xt init runs in /home/dawid/projects/mercury/{darth-feedor,terminalbeta}. NON_GOALS: no changes inside xtrm-tools repo itself. CONSTRAINTS: edits scoped to operator-level ops in consumer repos. VALIDATION: xt update --root ~/projects/mercury post-fix shows all repos refreshed (or already-current), no failed/incomplete. OUTPUT: cross-repo edit + xt init runs.

Filed memory about xt init not creating registry.json — future improvement candidate.

title: fix(cli): upsertManagedBlock orphan-marker bug duplicates xtrm:start blocks in AGENTS.md
description: PROBLEM: cli/src/commands/init.ts:upsertManagedBlock uses a single-line regex `${escapedStart}[\\s\\S]*?${escapedEnd}` to find and replace the managed block. When older versions of the template emitted a different marker format (or whitespace variation), the regex can fail to match the existing block and prepend a fresh one — leaving the old block + a free-floating end marker behind. Observed concretely in xtrm-tools/AGENTS.md: lines 1-150 contain xtrm:start...xtrm:end, and lines 152-256 contain a duplicate # XTRM Agent Workflow header that ends at an orphan xtrm:end on line 256 (no matching opening). The block content is functionally equivalent so agents reading the file still get the workflow, but the duplication is cosmetic noise that confuses git history. SUCCESS: upsertManagedBlock detects and removes orphan end markers / duplicate content before re-injecting; AGENTS.md has exactly one xtrm:start...xtrm:end block after running xt install / xt update. SCOPE: cli/src/commands/init.ts (upsertManagedBlock), cli/dist rebuild, regression test in cli/src/tests/ covering an input with an orphan end marker. NON_GOALS: do not change the marker format; do not touch XTRM-MANAGED:* blocks (those use a different marker scheme and are not affected). VALIDATION: targeted test passes; manual smoke — run xt install on a project with orphan markers, confirm AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md are deduplicated. OUTPUT: code fix + test + cleaned-up AGENTS.md in xtrm-tools. Post-release work.

(2) AGENTS.md has duplicate XTRM Agent Workflow content from upsertManagedBlock orphan-marker bug (xtrm:start...xtrm:end at 1-150 followed by free-floating duplicate ending at orphan xtrm:end on line 256). SUCCESS: (1) prepublishOnly --specialists-ref updated to 'master', (2) follow-up bead filed for the orphan-block bug. SCOPE: package.json (1-line change) + bd create. title: Install managed GitNexus post-commit analyze hook
description:
Problem: specialists runtime can auto-commit edit-capable specialist checkpoints, but GitNexus analyze should be triggered by a managed git post-commit hook owned by xtrm-tools, not hard-coded as specialists runtime policy. Current project hooks can be stale/absolute-path based, and no reliable xtrm-managed post-commit analyze hook is guaranteed.

Scope: xtrm-tools hook/template/install/update flow.

Requirements:
- Add/manage a local git post-commit hook that triggers GitNexus analyze after real commits.
- Preserve embeddings: if .gitnexus/meta.json indicates stats.embeddings > 0, run analyze with --embeddings; otherwise run normal analyze.
- Run non-blocking/detached with a lock to avoid concurrent analyze storms.
- Resolve repo root/worktree root correctly; avoid absolute paths to developer checkouts.
- Install/update idempotently through xt install/init/update-managed-assets path.
- Chain safely with existing git hooks rather than overwriting user hooks.
- Emit a discoverable log/status artifact so tools can prove the hook fired or failed.

Acceptance criteria:
- Fresh install has the managed post-commit hook without requiring a repo clone/symlink.
- A normal git commit triggers GitNexus analyze in the repo/worktree.
- Existing hook chains are preserved.
- Re-running xt update/install is idempotent.
- Tests or smoke script cover hook installation and a simulated post-commit invocation.

title: drift_detector: optional --enrich-with-gitnexus for semantic + impact-ranked drift
description:
PROBLEM: skills/updating-service-skills/scripts/drift_detector.py currently flags any territory file with mtime > last_sync. Most flagged entries are cosmetic (formatting, imports, comments) and don't warrant SKILL.md updates. On a real drift sweep this produces high noise — operators get a 50-entry list when only 5 are semantically meaningful. Has no notion of impact: every entry is flat, no ranking, no cross-territory signal.

SUCCESS:
- New optional flag '--enrich-with-gitnexus' on scan and check subcommands. Default off; tool functions identically to today when flag is absent or GitNexus is unreachable.
- When enabled and GitNexus index is fresh:
  - Per drifted file, call gitnexus_detect_changes (scoped to territory) to distinguish real symbol changes from cosmetic edits.
  - Per touched symbol, call gitnexus_impact(direction=upstream) to compute blast radius (callers, affected processes).
  - Output groups entries into 'high impact' / 'medium' / 'cosmetic' tiers with cited symbol names and affected process flows.
  - Cross-territory edge note: when a symbol from territory A is imported into territory B's territory file, surface as 'cross-territory drift signal' so the librarian considers updating both SKILLs.
- When GitNexus is unreachable or index is stale (gitnexus_detect_changes warns), tool emits a stderr line ('gitnexus enrichment skipped: <reason>') and falls through to mtime-only output. Never blocks.

SCOPE:
- skills/updating-service-skills/scripts/drift_detector.py — scan/check subcommands, output formatter.
- skills/creating-service-skills/scripts/bootstrap.py — small helper for GitNexus availability probe (importable; safe import; no top-level I/O).
- Tests: fresh project with fixture commits exercising cosmetic vs semantic edits; assert tier ranking; assert graceful fallback when GitNexus unavailable.

NON_GOALS:
- No replacement of mtime-based trigger. mtime stays primary; GitNexus is enrichment only.
- No automatic SKILL.md mutation; this remains the librarian's call.
- No GitNexus install or index management; tool only consumes if already available.
- No new env vars beyond optional GITNEXUS_DISABLE escape hatch.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Pure stdlib for the core path; GitNexus calls behind try/except so missing tools never break scan.
- Pre-flight check: gitnexus_detect_changes with timeout 2s; if it doesn't return cleanly, skip enrichment.
- Output remains parseable line-by-line so existing pipes/grep usage by operators keeps working; add structured fields after the existing 'service: file (last sync: ...)' line.

VALIDATION:
- pytest fixtures: cosmetic-only edit (returns mtime drift but enriched as 'cosmetic' tier); signature change (high tier, cites symbol + processes); cross-territory import (both services flagged, edge annotated).
- Live: run scan --enrich-with-gitnexus on darth-feedor immediately after a real session; expected: 50 mtime drift entries reduced to ~5-8 high-impact entries with the rest tagged cosmetic.

REFERENCES:
- Sibling bead xtrm-upm1 (registry-resolver fix). This bead depends on xtrm-upm1 because enrichment is moot if scan can't even find the registry.
- darth-feedor session 2026-05-07 motivated this — first real-world large-scale drift scan that produced too-noisy output to act on directly.
- gitnexus tool availability is broadly assumed across xtrm projects per CLAUDE.md conventions but not strictly required.
notes:
Implemented optional --enrich-with-gitnexus for scan path with 2s probe/fallback, tiered output fields, bootstrap GitNexus helpers; verified pytest+py_compile.
### Specialist Output — debugger (job 4d4b30) [WAITING — more output may follow]

Did: added optional `--enrich-with-gitnexus` flow in drift detector. Kept mtime primary. Added 2s GitNexus probe + hard fallback.

## Status

## Changes

  - `scan_drift(..., enrich_with_gitnexus=False)`
  - CLI flag parse: `scan --enrich-with-gitnexus`
  - probe via `is_gitnexus_available(timeout=2.0)`

title: service-registry resolver doesn't probe canonical xtrm pack layout
description:
PROBLEM: skills/creating-service-skills/scripts/bootstrap.py:get_registry_path probes only two locations: <root>/service-registry.json (preferred) and <root>/.claude/skills/service-registry.json (legacy). xtrm-tools itself ships service-registry.json under .xtrm/skills/user/packs/<pack>/service-registry.json (e.g. mercury pack on darth-feedor). Result: drift_detector.py scan returns 'No drift detected' WITHOUT erroring on any project that uses an xtrm pack — silent false-clean state. Confirmed live on darth-feedor 2026-05-07; required a manual symlink (ln -s .xtrm/skills/user/packs/mercury/service-registry.json service-registry.json) to make the detector functional. Same trap will hit any project that consumes an xtrm pack of service skills.

SUCCESS:
- get_registry_path additionally probes <root>/.xtrm/skills/user/packs/*/service-registry.json (sorted glob; first hit wins; if multiple exist, prefer one matching the project's active pack manifest if discoverable, else lexicographically first with a warning).
- Resolution order documented in module docstring updated.
- When NO registry is found, scan/sync subcommands print a one-line stderr hint pointing at expected paths (instead of silently returning empty).

SCOPE:
- skills/creating-service-skills/scripts/bootstrap.py — get_registry_path + module docstring.
- skills/updating-service-skills/scripts/drift_detector.py — scan/sync error path, only if it currently swallows the no-registry case.
- Mirror updates under .xtrm/skills/default/{creating,updating}-service-skills/ if those exist.

NON_GOALS:
- No new env var beyond existing SERVICE_REGISTRY_PATH override.
- No automatic registry creation.
- No symlink-management on consumer projects.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Backward-compatible: if root/legacy registry already exists, behavior unchanged.
- Pure stdlib (Path.glob).
- No I/O during import; resolution stays lazy.

VALIDATION:
- New unit test fixtures: project with xtrm pack-only registry; project with both root and xtrm pack (root wins); project with no registry (clear stderr).
- Live: drop into a fresh clone of darth-feedor (after removing the workaround symlink), run drift_detector.py scan — should find drift across the territories without manual setup.

REFERENCES:
- darth-feedor commit c7bdfb5 (workaround symlink) and bead py_backend-p5r.
- darth-feedor memory 'service-skills-drift-detector-silent-fail-mode-drift'.

title: Agent memory: context graphs, reasoning traces, entity extraction pipeline
description:
## Source

Distilled from: qMV64p-4Deo — 'Building Context Graphs for AI Agents' by Will Lyon, Neo4j (April 2026)
Analysis file: dev/omni-search-engine/docs/qMV64p-4Deo.analysis.md

## Core Concept: The Missing Why

A context graph is a knowledge graph that contains all information necessary to make decisions throughout a system — crucially including *why* decisions were made, not just what decisions were made.

Current xtrm agent sessions lose reasoning context between turns and sessions. Specialists produce outputs but don't record their reasoning traces in a queryable form.

## Three Memory Types (apply to xtrm specialist pipeline)

### 1. Short-term memory
Conversation/session state — the current turn's working context.
Already partially solved by bd prime + bead notes.

### 2. Long-term memory
Extracted entities and facts persisted across sessions.
Currently fragmented: bd memories, MEMORY.md files, bead notes — no unified graph.

### 3. Reasoning memory (MOST IMPORTANT — least supported)
Tool call traces, decision steps, token usage, specialist outputs — recorded as auditable history.
Currently: specialist results exist in job feeds and bead notes, but are not queryable or connected.

## Key Insight: Reasoning Memory as Precedent

When an agent makes a decision (e.g. executor chose approach X over Y because of Z), recording that decision in the memory graph makes it available as precedent for future similar decisions. The memory graph becomes self-improving.

Applied to xtrm: when a specialist closes a bead with --reason, that reason + the full decision context (what was tried, what failed, what worked) should be indexed as a retrievable precedent, not just a log entry.

## Entity Extraction Pipeline (cost-efficient)

Neo4j production approach — never LLM-first:
  1. spaCy / statistical NER (fast, free, CPU)
  2. GLINER 2 — local model, runs on CPU, fine-tuned for entity+relationship extraction
  3. LLM fallback ONLY when statistical methods fail

This is the right approach for extracting entities from specialist outputs, bead notes, and session logs without burning tokens on every entry.

## Proposed xtrm Integration Points

### A. bd remember enhancement
Current: bd remember 'insight' — flat key-value store
Proposed: tag entities from the insight automatically (people, tools, concepts, decisions)
so that bd memories 'keyword' can traverse entity relationships, not just keyword-match strings.

### B. Specialist reasoning traces
After each specialist run, extract the decision trace (tools called, findings, decisions made)
and store as a reasoning memory node linked to the bead.
Queryable: 'what did we try when fixing X?' returns tool call trace from that session.

### C. Precedent lookup before dispatch
Before dispatching an executor, check reasoning memory for similar past beads:
  bd precedent 'auth token refresh bug'  →  returns past decisions on similar tasks

### D. Session handoff context
When a new session starts, bd prime could surface not just active beads but relevant
reasoning memories from past sessions that match current bead topics.

## Implementation Notes

- Storage: extend the existing bd/dolt graph — reasoning memory as a new node type
- Extraction: run entity extraction on bead close (PostToolUse hook on bd close)
- No new infrastructure: leverage existing SQLite/dolt in .beads/
- GLINER 2 local model runs on CPU in seconds per document
- Start simple: capture bead close --reason text + specialist result summaries as first pass
notes: Release triage 2026-05-04: valuable roadmap work, but not required for the next patch release. Defer until after release-critical bugfix/docs/packaging items land.

title: RFC: global pi extensions with project-level manifest validation
description:
## Problem

Per-project pi extension management via .pi/settings.json → .xtrm/extensions/ symlinks is fragile. Symlinks break across worktrees, pi-core disappears requiring xt pi reload repair cycles, and every worktree bootstrap needs to re-establish the symlink tree. This was identified during hgpu (worktree-per-executor isolation) work in the specialists repo — the worktree Pi bootstrap step exists solely because of this fragility.

## Current design

Extensions installed per-project under .xtrm/extensions/node_modules/. .pi/settings.json points to project-local paths. Worktrees need symlinks (.pi → main/.pi) to share the install. Symlinks break → RPC timeout → xt pi reload to repair.

## Proposed design

Global install, project-level opt-in manifest:

1. Extensions installed globally at ~/.pi/agent/extensions/ (many already are via npm)
2. Project declares requirements in a manifest (e.g. .pi/extensions.json or pi.extensions in package.json):
   ```json
   {
     "required": ["pi-gitnexus", "pi-serena-tools", "@xtrm/pi-core"],
     "optional": ["pi-mcp-adapter"]
   }
   ```
3. On pi startup: validate globals satisfy manifest, warn/error if missing
4. Project-level overrides still possible via .xtrm/extensions/ but only when explicitly needed (dev/testing)

## Benefits

- Worktrees just work — no symlinks, no bootstrap, no repair
- Traceability preserved — manifest is committed, teammates see what's needed
- Skills control preserved — manifest declares intent, pi validates
- Simpler xt pi/xt claude bootstrap (skip symlink step entirely)
- hgpu worktree Pi bootstrap becomes a no-op

## Costs

- Can't pin project-specific extension versions (all projects share global version)
- Extension dev/testing slightly harder (need explicit local override)
- Migration from current per-project installs

## Origin

Discovered during specialists repo hgpu implementation (worktree isolation feature). Pi-core symlink broke repeatedly between specialist runs, causing 10s RPC timeouts. The fragility is the direct cause of the Pi bootstrap requirement in the hgpu worktree design.
close_reason: Implemented: global extensions with repo symlinks. Renamed .xtrm/extensions/ → .xtrm/ext-src/ to avoid Pi auto-discovery conflicts. Extensions symlinked from ~/.pi/agent/extensions/ → .xtrm/ext-src/. Edit in repo works globally.

title: Add conflict resolution policy for global vs local skill scope in registry spec
description: Reviewer gap from xtrm-s3pf (91/100): docs/skills-registry-exploration.md does not cover what happens when a skill exists in both global and local registry. Add a subsection covering: version pinning, precedence rules, user-modified managed skills, and conflict resolution policy.
notes:
Updated: `docs/skills-registry-exploration.md`

I added and strengthened the **“Scope conflict policy (global vs local registry)”** subsection to explicitly cover:

- **Version pinning policy**
  - exact pin expectations for managed skills
  - no cross-scope semver-max behavior
  - required effective-output fields

- **Precedence rules**
  - deterministic order: local explicit > global > bundled default
  - local explicit disable behavior
  - scope-flag semantics vs runtime precedence
  - worktree override placement

- **User-modified managed skills**
  - immutable managed payloads
  - overlay model keyed by skill/base version
  - drift handling + upgrade overlay reapply behavior
  - mismatch → conflict behavior

- **Conflict resolution algorithm**
  - explicit step-by-step resolution flow
  - standardized statuses (`resolved-*`, `drifted`, `conflict`)
  - fail-closed per-skill policy
  - `xt skills doctor` remediation command expectations
  - idempotent decision recording

title: Define three-tier packaging model (default/extra/user) formally in skills registry spec
description: Reviewer gap from xtrm-s3pf (91/100): The default/extra/user tier model is referenced in docs/skills-registry-exploration.md but not formally defined. Create an explicit schema: what assets populate default (npm bundle), extra (optional packs), and user (local overlays), with precedence rules and conflict resolution policy between tiers.
notes:
Implemented ✅ in:

- `docs/skills-registry-exploration.md`

### What I added

- A **formal three-tier model** section for `default / extra / user`
- A **normative registry schema** for tier metadata
- Explicit **asset record contracts**:
  - `ManagedAsset` for `default` + `extra`
  - `UserOverlayAsset` for `user`
- Clear rules for what populates each tier:
  - `default`: npm/plugin bundled managed assets
  - `extra`: optional enabled packs
  - `user`: local overlays/custom skills
- Deterministic **precedence**: `user > extra > default`
- Formal **conflict policy** with fail-closed semantics per skill
- Deterministic **extra-pack collision tie-break**:
  1. explicit decision record
  2. lowest `pack.priority`
  3. lexical `packId`
- Requirement to persist winner decisions for idempotent reruns

Also claimed and closed bead `xtrm-ipas` with memory ack + memory entry.

title: [epic] Project utils system: typed utility scripts, manifest, and process manager
description:
## Overview

Introduce a first-class `utils/` system to xtrm-tools — a set of typed, manifested utility scripts that specialists and orchestrators can invoke declaratively, plus an abstract process manager module for long-lived/background processes.

## Architecture

Xtreme is the execution substrate (worktrees, process lifecycle, toolchain, hooks). This epic formalizes that role with concrete tooling.

### Layer boundary (to document formally)

- **Xtreme** = provisioning, worktrees, process lifecycle, session management, toolchain, shared scripts, pre/post hooks
- **Specialist** = role logic, task execution, output, handoff — consumes Xtreme primitives

## Planned sub-epics / bundles

### 1. Utils folder + typed manifest (foundation)
- `utils/manifest.json` schema: id, description, runtime, command, input, output, timeout, phase (pre/post/on-demand), failure_policy (blocking/advisory)
- Every utility declares its contract; no ad-hoc scripts

### 2. Utility script bundles (implement manifest contract)
- **Core** — health-check, lint, type-check, format-check, test-smoke
- **Validators** — schema-validator (JSON/YAML/specialist-output), config-validator (env, project config), policy-validator (naming, structure, ownership)
- **Security** — secret-scan, dependency-vulnerability-check, unsafe-pattern-scan
- **Git/change intelligence** — changed-files-analyzer (classifies by type), diff-risk-estimator (coupling + missing tests), branch-consistency-check
- **Testing** — targeted-test-selector (runs tests covering changed files only), coverage-delta-check, flaky-test-detector
- **Observability** — log-follow-helper, error-summarizer, incident-snapshot (processes + ports + logs + repo state bundle)
- **Docker** — docker-health, docker-compose-validate, image-policy-check

### 3. Specialist integration
- Expose utils manifest to specialist YAML via `capabilities.utils[]`
- Specialists declare which utils to run at which phase (pre/in/post)

### 4. Abstract process manager module
- Interface: start / stop / restart / status / logs / follow / cleanup
- PNPM-backed as first implementation; interface-abstracted for future runtimes
- Named processes, PID + log tracking, follow streaming, reattach to existing
- Process policies: timeout, restart policy, blocking vs background classification
- Automatic cleanup at job end
- Enables specialists to launch dev servers, log tails, event streams, watchers — and human/agent operators to follow with `--follow`

## Sequencing

1. Architecture doc (formal Xtreme vs Specialist boundary)
2. Utils manifest schema
3. Core + Validator bundles (highest utility for specialists)
4. Process manager (unlocks background process UX)
5. Remaining bundles + specialist integration

## Notes

This epic is intentionally coarse-grained. Each sub-item above becomes its own issue when the time comes to implement it. File this as a planning anchor — break it down before starting.
notes: Release triage 2026-05-04: valuable roadmap work, but not required for the next patch release. Defer until after release-critical bugfix/docs/packaging items land.

title: Serena: expose dump-context tool for compact-save integration
description: compact-save can only save what's on disk. Serena's in-session symbol context (recently read files, open symbols, LSP state) lives in MCP server memory and is lost on /compact. To preserve this, Serena would need a tool like dump_session_context that writes current file/symbol state to a file (e.g. .serena/.session-context.json). compact-save could then snapshot it; compact-restore could inject it as additionalSystemPrompt. Upstream ask or local Serena wrapper.
close_reason: Deferred — upstream Serena feature, no implementation path without upstream support.

title: compact-save: include Serena project re-activation hint in restore
description: After /compact, Serena's activated project is lost (MCP server memory is cleared). The restore hook currently only reinstates beads in_progress status. It should also detect if a .serena/ config exists in the project cwd, record the project name in .beads/.last_active bundle, and inject an additionalSystemPrompt on restore like 'Serena was active on project X — re-activate with activate_project before any symbol lookups.' This is a pure shell change: check for .serena/config.yml or .serena/ dir in save hook, add serenaProject field to the JSON bundle, emit reminder in restore hook.
close_reason: Done: save hook detects .serena/project.yml and stores serenaProject in bundle; restore hook injects re-activation reminder in additionalSystemPrompt

title: Suppress duplicate xtrm-ui extension conflict noise in Pi
description: When running Pi from within the xtrm-tools project, Pi auto-discovers config/pi/extensions/xtrm-ui/index.ts via the 'pi' key in its package.json, AND also loads the globally installed ~/.pi/agent/extensions/xtrm-ui/. This causes noisy conflict warnings at startup (tool/command conflicts). The project-local version wins so behavior is correct, but the warnings are ugly. Options: (1) remove the global copy since project-local always wins in this repo, (2) find a way to suppress auto-discovery for the project-local source copy, or (3) check if Pi 0.63.1 has a way to exclude paths from auto-discovery.
close_reason: xtrm-ui work complete: pi-dex parity shipped, Serena MCP compaction merged (xtrm-oh7l), GitNexus deferred pending Pi API

title: Derive canonical lists dynamically in clean command
description:
In clean.ts, canonical hooks and skills are hardcoded:

```typescript
const CANONICAL_HOOKS = new Set([...]);
const CANONICAL_SKILLS = new Set([...]);
```

These lists must be manually kept in sync with actual repo contents. If a new skill is added but forgotten in this list, xtrm clean will delete it.

**Impact**: Data loss if new skills/hooks are added without updating the hardcoded lists.

**Recommendation**: Derive canonical lists dynamically from repo contents (scan hooks/ and skills/ directories).
close_reason: Stale: CLI Bugfixes Audit from 2026-03-17 no longer relevant

title: Feature: Remove Turn counter from statusline
description:
## Problem

The Turn counter in the statusline () provides no actionable value. Users don't need to track conversation turn count — it's internal state that doesn't inform decision-making.

## Current Display

```
XTRM [Turn 3] | claude-sonnet | 12% | ⌂ xtrm-tools | ⎇ feature/foo
```

## Proposed Display

```
XTRM | claude-sonnet | 12% | ⌂ xtrm-tools | ⎇ feature/foo
```

## Changes

### File: `config/pi/extensions/custom-footer.ts`

Remove:
1. `let turnCount = 0;` variable
2. `pi.on("turn_start", ...)` handler
3. `pi.on("session_switch", ...)` handler
4. `const turns = theme.fg("dim", "[Turn ${turnCount}]");` in render
5. Reference to `turns` in the output string

### Before

```typescript
let turnCount = 0;

pi.on("turn_start", async () => {
  turnCount++;
});

// In render:
const turns = theme.fg("dim", "[Turn ${turnCount}]");

const leftParts = [`${brand} ${turns}`, modelStr, usageStr, cwdStr];
```

### After

```typescript
// In render:
const leftParts = [brand, modelStr, usageStr, cwdStr];
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Turn counter removed from statusline
- [ ] No `turnCount` variable or handlers in custom-footer.ts
- [ ] Layout still balanced without the Turn element

## Related

- Issue: xtrm-6g0 (beads statusline integration — will add chip in same area)
close_reason: Implemented in 1ed9c54 (feat/statusline beads bold #71)

title: Eval: consolidate beads hook files vs extract shared utils
description:
## Context

This project is **xtrm-tools** — a collection of Claude Code agent skills, hooks, and tooling for a multi-agent development workflow. Hooks are shell scripts (mostly .mjs) that fire at lifecycle events in Claude Code sessions (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, etc.) and enforce workflow rules.

## The Hooks in Question

Four beads hooks live in `~/.claude/hooks/` (installed globally, not tracked in this repo):

| File | Event | Purpose |
|------|-------|---------|
| `beads-edit-gate.mjs` | PreToolUse (Write/Edit) | Blocks file edits if this session has no claimed issue |
| `beads-stop-gate.mjs` | Stop | Blocks agent stop if this session has an unclaimed issue |
| `beads-commit-gate.mjs` | PreToolUse (Bash/git commit) | Blocks git commit if this session has an unclaimed issue |
| `beads-close-memory-prompt.mjs` | PostToolUse (Bash/bd close) | Clears kv claim + injects reminder after bd close |

## How They Work (Recent Refactor)

All three gate hooks now use **session-scoped claiming via `bd kv`** instead of global in_progress checks:

```js
// Shared pattern in all three gate hooks (currently duplicated):
function getSessionClaim(sessionId) {
  try {
    return execSync(`bd kv get "claimed:${sessionId}"`, { ... }).trim();
  } catch (err) {
    if (err.status === 1) return '';  // key not found
    return null;                      // bd kv unavailable — fail open
  }
}
```

Each hook also duplicates: exec options boilerplate, `.beads` dir check, cwd resolution, and the fallback path (no session_id → old global in_progress check).

## The Question for Evaluation

Two options were proposed:

### Option A — Single dispatcher script
One `beads-gate.mjs` file handles all events, routes internally by inspecting `input.hook_type` or similar.

**Key constraint**: Claude Code does NOT pass the hook event type in the hook input JSON. So `settings.json` still needs separate entries per event — each pointing to the same file. The file would need another mechanism to know which path to take (e.g. a CLI flag, or env var set per-entry in settings.json, e.g. `BEADS_HOOK_MODE=edit node beads-gate.mjs`).

### Option B — Shared utils module
Keep four separate hook files. Extract duplicated logic into `beads-gate-utils.mjs`:
- `getSessionClaim(sessionId, cwd)`
- `execBd(cmd, cwd)`
- `hasTotalWork(cwd)`
- `checkBeadsProject(cwd)`

Each hook imports from utils. Files stay small and independently testable.

## What We Want You to Evaluate

1. Is Option A actually viable given the Claude Code hook input constraint (no event type in JSON)? Is there a clean way to pass routing context per-entry in settings.json?
2. Is Option B the pragmatic choice, or does it add complexity for modest gain?
3. Is there an Option C we haven't considered?
4. What's the blast radius / maintenance risk of each approach over time, as more hooks get added?

Please post your recommendation as a comment or note on this issue. The author will implement based on your answer.
close_reason: Implemented: shared utils extracted, all hooks updated, withSafeBdContext added

comments.author: you@example.com
comments.text:
Recommendation: choose Option B now (separate hook entrypoints + shared utils module), with a narrow Option C fallback if/when complexity grows.

1) Option A viability
- Technically viable only if you pass explicit mode per settings entry (CLI arg or env var), since hook JSON does not include event type.
- That means you still keep multiple settings entries, and now add a dispatch contract that can drift (mode string mismatch, missing arg/env).
- Net: centralization gain is smaller than it appears, while routing fragility increases.

2) Option B pragmatism
- Best current tradeoff. Keep event-specific scripts for clarity and independent behavior, extract only duplicated primitives:
  - resolveCwd/input parsing
  - checkBeadsProject
  - execBd/getSessionClaim
  - fallback global in_progress path
- This removes repetition without coupling unrelated policy decisions into one dispatcher.

3) Option C (recommended guardrail)
- Hybrid: Option B + one tiny shared guard wrapper for uniform error/timeout/fail-open policy (e.g., withSafeBdContext(fn)).
- Keep policy logic in each hook, infra logic in utils. Avoid full dispatcher until >~8 hooks or heavy shared branching emerges.

4) Blast radius / maintenance risk
- Option A: high blast radius. A dispatcher bug or routing regression can break all hook events at once.
- Option B: low-to-medium blast radius. Utils bugs affect many hooks, but event logic stays isolated and easier to test.
- Option C hybrid: medium, but controlled if shared wrapper is very small and stable.

Decision rule going forward
- Stay on Option B while hooks remain mostly independent.
- Reconsider dispatcher only when duplication is primarily in policy branching (not just shell/bd plumbing) and you can enforce strict mode validation + tests per event mode.

title: Investigate: bd create may auto-set in_progress, triggering stop gate
description: After claiming an issue (bd update --status=in_progress + bd kv set), the stop gate showed in_progress on a freshly-created issue before any explicit status change. Possible causes: bd create defaults to in_progress status, or beads-edit-gate's bd kv claiming logic has a side effect on issue state. Reproduce: create a fresh issue, check status immediately, observe if stop gate fires. Compare behavior with and without the new session-scoped kv claiming code.
notes: ROOT CAUSE FOUND: Not bd create defaulting to in_progress (confirmed: new issues are always created as OPEN). The stop gate false-positive is a Dolt read inconsistency: bd list --status=in_progress reads the Dolt *working set* while bd show reads the *committed* state. With dolt.auto-commit=off (default), bd update writes to the working set immediately but bd close may only commit after the fact. Result: bd list sees stale in_progress from the working set even though bd show reports CLOSED from HEAD. The new session-scoped stop gate (kv claim check) sidesteps this entirely — it won't fire for a session with no kv claim, regardless of what bd list shows. Mitigation: ensure bd close always flushes the working set, OR document that dolt.auto-commit=on is required for gate consistency.
close_reason: Diagnosed: Dolt working-set vs committed-state read inconsistency, not bd create behavior

title: Add PreCompact/SessionStart hooks to restore in_progress beads state after compaction
description:
After /compact, beads issue statuses persist in Dolt but the agent resumes without knowing which issues were in_progress. The gate fires on the first write and the agent must manually re-mark issues before continuing.

Fix: ship two hooks as part of xtrm-tools (installed via xtrm install into ~/.claude/hooks/):

1. PreCompact hook (new): hooks/beads-compact-save.py (or .cjs)
   - Runs bd list --status=in_progress
   - Writes issue IDs to .beads/.last_active (one ID per line)
   - Fires before context is compacted so the list is accurate

2. SessionStart hook addition: extend existing hooks or add hooks/beads-compact-restore.cjs
   - On session start, checks for .beads/.last_active
   - If found, runs bd update <id> --status=in_progress for each ID
   - Deletes .beads/.last_active after restore
   - Injects a brief context message: 'Restored N in_progress issues from last session'

Both hooks wired in config/settings.json and synced by xtrm install.

Note: beads-edit-gate.mjs is owned by the beads npm package and should not be modified directly. Our hooks complement it without patching upstream. If the beads package ever ships its own compaction support, our hooks can be retired.

Acceptance criteria:
- /compact followed by new session → first write is NOT blocked by the gate
- Issues that were in_progress before compact are still in_progress after
- .beads/.last_active is cleaned up after restore (idempotent)
close_reason: Closed

title: Register hello command in cli/src/index.ts
description:
## Context

Part of the xtrm-hello epic (xtrm-wdt7). Depends on xtrm-wdt7.1 (the command module must exist first). This is the shell/wiring layer task: import the factory and add it to the Commander program.

## What to build

Edit \`cli/src/index.ts\`:
1. Add import: \`import { createHelloCommand } from './commands/hello.js';\`
2. Add to program: \`program.addCommand(createHelloCommand());\`

Place the import alongside the other command imports (alphabetical order near the help/install block). Place \`addCommand\` call in the same region as the other \`addCommand\` calls.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] \`xtrm hello\` (after build) prints \`Hello\` and exits 0
- [ ] \`xtrm --help\` lists \`hello\` in the commands section
- [ ] Build succeeds: \`cd cli && npm run build\`
- [ ] No lint errors

## Approach notes

- File to edit: \`cli/src/index.ts\`
- Layer: **shell** (wires the factory into the CLI program)
- All other commands follow the same addCommand pattern — just copy the style
close_reason: Implemented hello command with Commander.js factory pattern, registered in index.ts, unit + integration tests all passing

title: Implement hello command — cli/src/commands/hello.ts
description:
## Context

Part of the xtrm-hello epic (xtrm-wdt7). This is the core layer task: create the command module that contains the pure output logic. No I/O dependencies beyond stdout; no HTTP, no DB, no filesystem reads.

## What to build

Create \`cli/src/commands/hello.ts\` exporting \`createHelloCommand(): Command\` that:
- Uses Commander.js \`new Command('hello')\`
- Sets description: \`'Say hello and stop'\`
- In the \`.action()\` handler: calls \`console.log('Hello')\` and returns (Commander handles exit)

Follow the pattern from \`cli/src/commands/help.ts\` (same shape: factory function, no external deps).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] File \`cli/src/commands/hello.ts\` exists and compiles (\`cd cli && npx tsc --noEmit\`)
- [ ] \`createHelloCommand()\` returns a valid Commander Command
- [ ] Running the action prints exactly \`Hello\` to stdout
- [ ] No lint errors (\`npm run lint\` clean)

## Approach notes

- Pattern to follow: \`cli/src/commands/help.ts\` — minimal factory, no imports except Commander
- Naming: \`createHelloCommand\` matches the \`create<Name>Command\` convention used everywhere
- Layer: **core** (pure logic, no I/O side effects beyond console.log)
close_reason: Implemented hello command with Commander.js factory pattern, registered in index.ts, unit + integration tests all passing

title: xtrm hello: print hello and stop
description:
## Overview

Add a minimal \`xtrm hello\` command that prints \"Hello\" to stdout and exits cleanly. This demonstrates the command scaffold pattern and serves as a working reference for future minimal commands.

## Goals

- Goal 1: \`xtrm hello\` prints \"Hello\" and exits with code 0
- Goal 2: Command follows existing Commander.js factory pattern

## Non-goals

- No flags, no formatting, no i18n
- Not a real user-facing feature — reference/demo only

## Success criteria

- [ ] \`xtrm hello\` outputs exactly \"Hello\" to stdout
- [ ] Exit code is 0
- [ ] Command appears in \`xtrm --help\`
- [ ] Tests pass, lint clean

## Context / background

Commander.js CLI, commands live in cli/src/commands/, registered in cli/src/index.ts.

value: custom-footer now mirrors statusline multi-claim logic by parsing bd list --status=in_progress with deduped /^◐\s+([a-z][\w-]+)/gm regex; single claim fetches title via bd show, multi-claim renders id-only chips

Pi extension packages require 'pi install -l <path>' registration after copy - fs.copy alone doesn't make pi discover them. Also: two sources of truth exist: config/pi/install-schema.json (installer) vs package.json → pi.packages (runtime). Keep both in sync.

Worktree commit sequence: beads commit gate fires in worktrees too. Correct order: (1) close beads with memory ack, (2) commit in worktree, (3) run reviewer, (4) merge into main. Never try to commit in a worktree while the parent bead is still in_progress.

xt init/pi runtime now ensures global install of npm:pi-gitnexus and npm:pi-serena-tools (idempotent check in ~/.pi/agent/npm/node_modules) so settings.json declarations can’t silently leave Serena/GitNexus unavailable.

xt install now scaffolds project .xtrm directly from package .xtrm/registry.json with SHA-256 drift detection; drifted files are skipped by default and only overwritten with --force (with confirmation unless -y).

dist rebuild required: whenever install-project.ts or any CLI source is changed, cli/dist/ must be rebuilt (npm run build in cli/) and committed. CI checks 'Verify dist is up to date' and fails if dist is stale.

Plan-mode extension: bd integration requires spawnSync for synchronous commands. Widget updates with issueIds Map for tracking bd issue per plan step. Use Array.from(matchAll) for TypeScript compatibility without downlevelIteration.

XTRM-Guide framing: xtrm-tools is a dual-runtime system — Claude Code plugin AND Pi extension suite. Policy compiler is the shared source; both runtimes are peers. Architecture diagram should show policy compiler at top feeding Claude Code and Pi in parallel.

Cat B distribution work should consolidate to single canonical owner before adding runtime multi-source resolution; current xtrm-tools already has registry-based copy sync plus optional/user pack machinery, so new epic scope should target simplification and stale-model cleanup first.
service-registry resolver must probe .xtrm/skills/user/packs/* and drift scan must print missing-registry hint to avoid silent false-clean
xtrm-4amc5: cli/hooks test expects direct script entries in .xtrm/config/hooks.json; keep beads-compact hooks as additive direct entries so test wiring can detect them.

beads Pi session key: use process.pid.toString() not ctx.sessionManager.sessionId — PID is stable for Pi process lifetime. KV key: claimed:<pid>. custom-footer busts cache (lastFetch=0) via tool_result on bd close/update/create/claim + 200ms delayed refresh. requestRender captured from tui in setFooter callback to trigger repaints from async refresh.

Conflict-chain integration: when integration branch already fully contains fix branch, do not reset onto main to resolve overlapping specialist chains. Keep integration branch as staging area, resolve by semantic cluster, and treat old-branch patches that predate canonical layout pivots (flat active view, pack-first service-skill registry) as re-execute-or-supersede rather than raw cherry-pick targets.
xtrm-gl8b.5: settings template should omit hooks/plugins; installer injects canonical hooks from .xtrm/config/hooks.json with absolute .xtrm/hooks script paths via ConfigAdapter.
never use --auto flag for gh pr merge. merge directly or wait for CI and flag the issue to the user instead. auto-merge is non-transparent.

plugin-cache-architecture-github-source: xtrm-tools plugin cache structure with github source: cache/0.5.10/ has hooks/, skills/, .mcp.json as symlinks to marketplaces/xtrm-tools/ (always live). .claude-plugin/ was a stale copy — replaced with symlink to marketplaces/xtrm-tools/.claude-plugin/ so version reads are always current. installed_plugins.json version field is what update checks compare against. autoUpdate:true in known_marketplaces.json pulls from GitHub at startup. Full version tracking flow: npm version patch → sync-cli-version.mjs updates plugin.json → git push → autoUpdate pulls → version mismatch detected → update offered.

xt doctor global Pi package health: xtrm-modr adds piPackages to doctor JSON/text, reports missing/outdated/version-unknown for canonical xt-managed Pi packages, never installs from doctor, and bounds npm version lookup with timeout plus visible offline warning.
hooks/hooks.json for Claude Code plugin must wrap all events in an outer 'hooks' key — same format as config/hooks.json. Flat event map fails claude plugin validate.

Parallel specialist chains that touch shared files (cli/src/commands/{install,update,doctor}.ts, scripts/vendor-specialists-skills.mjs, .github/workflows/ci.yml, scripts/gen-registry.mjs, skills/.../bootstrap.py) conflict on integration cherry-pick at roughly 40-50%. 2026-05-09 session: 12/20 chains landed clean; 8 conflicted. Mitigation: dispatch known-overlapping chains in serial (one at a time), OR accept that integration phase needs debugger-resolves-conflict pass per overlap, OR squash chains within an epic via planner before parallel implementation. The orchestrator should map overlap surface BEFORE dispatching the wave.

pi-processes-pnpm-prereq: @aliou/pi-processes added to all Pi package lists (install-schema.json, settings.json.template, package.json pi.packages). install-pi.ts now checks/installs pnpm as a prerequisite via ensurePnpm() — pi-processes encourages pnpm dev/build/test commands so pnpm must be available.
xt doctor/init/clean runtime-check strings/tests must target flat .xtrm/skills/active and expose deprecated agents path flag in doctor JSON

Pi extensions in config/pi/extensions/ need package.json with 'exports' field for Pi to load them. Install locally with 'pi install ./path -l' which adds to .pi/settings.json. Without package.json, extensions won't load even if code exists.

Pi global package assurance: ensureAlwaysGlobalPiPackages now iterates getXtManagedPiPackages, so all canonical xt Pi packages are checked in the global Pi agent npm tree rather than trusting project Pi settings or a two-package allowlist.

specialists parity gaps vs xtrm-tools (2026-03-20): (1) unitAI-lmi: worktree Dolt bootstrap required for spawned Pi sessions — same trick as xtrm worktrees. (2) unitAI-4az: beads-compact-save/restore hooks missing from specialists. (3) unitAI-200: beads-claim-sync auto-commit missing from specialists. (4) unitAI-o6j + unitAI-0x9 still reference deprecated beads-close-memory-prompt.mjs — should point to beads-memory-gate.mjs Stop hook pattern. (5) unitAI-pjx (memory judgment gate) is the specialists equivalent of beads-memory-gate.mjs.
sync-docs scripts only validate docs/*.md (glob, not rglob). Subdirs like docs/plans/, docs/reference/ are ignored. ~/.agents/skills/ is global install; skills/ in repo is source for xtrm-tools.

creating-service-skills now resolves canonical pack roots from XTRM_PACK or single-pack auto-detect, stores registry under .xtrm/skills/user/packs/<pack>/service-registry.json, and scaffolds legacy .claude/skills symlinks for backward compatibility.

xt-end-no-commits-pr-fails: xt end fails GraphQL 'No commits between main and xt/branch' when worktree commit was already absorbed into origin/main — git reset --hard origin/main before xt end, or skip PR and clean up manually

xtrm-1c03 extension parity harness drift fixed: tests mock packages/pi-extensions/src/core, use clearAllMocks to keep implementations, runtime untouched, extension suite passes. Remaining xtrm-ok7c clusters are C4 clean orphan detection, C6 registry optional realpath, C9 pi launch self-heal.

xtrm-own9.4: init phase 6 no longer delegates to runInstall; it now calls installFromRegistry + scaffoldSkillsDefaultFromPackage + runPiInstall + ensureAgentsSkillsSymlink directly, with optional prune cleanup preserved via runPluginEraCleanup.
xtrm-ul5a review: release command landed but parity/scope-guard proof incomplete; require explicit guard tests and publish parity check

for xtrm-managed skills, updating only the live local copy is insufficient; future installs/syncs source the canonical repo copy under skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md, so both layers must be aligned or local improvements can be silently reverted

gitnexus-hook.cjs PostToolUse matcher now includes Grep|Read|Glob in addition to Bash and Serena tools — full parity with Pi extension auto-augmentation. Source: policies/gitnexus.json. Compiled to hooks/hooks.json via node scripts/compile-policies.mjs.

worktree-session now suppresses .beads noise by appending .beads to per-worktree git info/exclude and skipping tracked .beads paths after symlink swap; keeps bd hook routing intact while avoiding phantom deletions in checkpoint commits
xt report generate should use capped file lists and session-scoped specialist metadata instead of diff stat dumps

bd worktree isolation: EnterWorktree auto-spawns a separate Dolt server on a new random port with its own empty db. Fix: bd dolt stop && echo '13669' > .beads/dolt-server.port to redirect to main's running server. xtpi must pre-configure this on worktree creation — sessions cannot migrate from main to worktree mid-session.

xtrm-42in strict registry audit: xt update --apply ignores XTRM_STRICT_REGISTRY env because only CLI flag forwards strictRegistry into runInstall; CI using env-only on update can bypass mismatch gate.

xtrm-init-bootstrapProjectInit-docker-removed: bootstrapProjectInit() no longer calls detectProjectFeatures for docker services or ensureServiceRegistry. Docker service detection/registry scaffolding removed — there is a service-skills skill for that. init now only does: bd init, injectProjectInstructionHeaders, runGitNexusInitForProject, detect TS/Python for summary output.

Claude hooks (hooks/*.mjs) are symlinked to plugins/xtrm-tools/hooks for plugin distribution. Pi extensions (config/pi/extensions/*.ts) are separate TypeScript code that runs in Pi's extension runtime. Both need parallel implementations for workflow features like autocommit.

pi-mcp-adapter (v2.2.1) works via Pi RPC mode. Key: use npm package install (not local extension), args param is JSON string not object. Lazy lifecycle verified: servers connect on-demand, metadata cache enables search without connections. Test servers: @modelcontextprotocol/server-everything (reference), @upstash/context7-mcp (real-world docs lookup).

npx-skills-cli-non-interactive: npx skills find launches interactive TUI (blocks agents). Use 'npx skills market-search <query> --limit N' for non-interactive marketplace search. Also: 'npx skills list' (local), 'npx skills show <name>' (detail), 'npx skills market-install <name>' (install). The 'find' subcommand is agent-hostile.

skills-directory-symlink-approach: .claude/skills and .agents/skills are directory-level symlinks to .xtrm/skills/default (real dir). gitnexus analyze writes .xtrm/skills/default/gitnexus/ through the symlink. Registry copies individual skill files to .xtrm/skills/default making it a real writable dir (scaffoldSkillsDefaultFromPackage removed). ensureAgentsSkillsSymlink() in init.ts creates both symlinks in Phase 6b before gitnexus init. Pi .settings.json skills key points to ../.xtrm/skills/default.

docs-cross-check-test-finding-condition: test/docs-cross-check.test.ts 'shows severity labels for findings' used includes('finding') which matched '0 findings' (zero count). Fixed to /[1-9]\d* finding/ regex. Pattern: always use a count-aware regex when branching on presence of findings/errors/results in CLI output tests.

Updated plugin-era prune to remove only xtrm-tools plugin artifacts and preserve other Claude plugins; added machine-bootstrap step to ensure serena/context7 are installed idempotently via claude plugin install.

xtrm install --prune (xtrm-fin): hooks in deepMergeWithProtection are NOT blocked by PROTECTED_KEYS — they go through mergeHooksObject first (key === 'hooks' branch, continues before protection check). The real drift source is mergeMatcher accumulating stale tokens and pruneStaleWrappers not catching scripts in wrong events. Fix: replaceHooksObject() in prune mode replaces xtrm-managed wrappers wholesale per event, preserving user-local hooks (commands not from ~/.claude/hooks/).

Pi package freshness helper design: getManagedPiPackageFreshness in cli/src/core/pi-runtime.ts is intentionally provider-injected and network-free; command beads should supply installed/expected version providers and consume states missing/current/outdated/version-unknown.

Recovering xtrm-tools/.beads after the PR #196 self-symlink bug: (1) rm .beads (the symlink), (2) git checkout <pre-#196-commit> -- .beads (e.g. e3a260b), (3) chmod 700 .beads, (4) ensure 'dolt.shared-server: true' is in .beads/config.yaml (matches all other projects on this machine), (5) bd doctor --fix --yes to clean gitignores, (6) bd hooks install --force to refresh hooks, (7) bd bootstrap rehydrates the dolt DB from issues.jsonl. Sensitive files (.beads-credential-key, interactions.jsonl) must NOT be re-added — they auto-regenerate. Database lives at ~/.beads/shared-server/dolt/jaggers_agent_tools — local .beads/dolt is unused under shared-server mode.

Added cli install integration tests covering fresh scaffold, idempotent re-install, drift skip/force behavior, dry-run no-write behavior, and settings hook merge preservation. Also adjusted install drift logging to suppress warnings under --force for expected overwrite mode.
cli help text changes in src need cli/dist rebuild before CLI help tests reflect updated description; init root-resolution errors should include original cause text to satisfy existing assertions.

Global Pi extensions with repo symlinks work well. Key insight: ~/.pi/agent/extensions/node_modules/@xtrm/pi-core symlink is needed for extension resolution. Skills should not duplicate what npm packages provide (gitnexus-* from pi-gitnexus). Worktree bootstrap reduced from ~40 lines to ~10 lines (only .pi/npm cache sharing).
xt skills enable now auto-syncs PACK.json skills[] from discovered pack filesystem entries before invariant checks, resolving PACK_METADATA_MISMATCH when skills were added manually.

pack-aware scaffolder audit: XTRM_PACK and service_id both feed filesystem paths without containment checks; ensure_legacy_symlink can delete attacker-selected dirs before replacing with symlink. Treat CLI scaffolder inputs as untrusted local input.

hooks/hooks.json must be committed to git — it is the compiled output of policies/*.json and is required by the plugin at install time. If accidentally deleted, regenerate with: node scripts/compile-policies.mjs

Removed plugin-era runtime artifacts; Claude runtime verification/worktree flow now rely on .xtrm hooks wired through .claude/settings.json, and sync-cli-version no longer depends on .claude-plugin files.

sync-docs --fix run 2026-03-18: created 5 scaffold docs/ files (hooks.md, pi-extensions.md, mcp-servers.md, policies.md, skills.md); injected YAML frontmatter into 7 existing schema-invalid files (cleanup.md, delegation-architecture.md, hook-system-summary.md, mcp-servers-config.md, pi-extensions-migration.md, pre-install-cleanup.md, todo.md). All 12/12 docs/ files now pass validate_doc.py. Remaining: CHANGELOG stale (v2.0.0 vs package.json v2.4.0) and README has 5 extraction candidates.

gl8b-implementation-phases: xtrm-gl8b broken into 12 child beads by planner (2026-04-01). Phase P0=foundation: .1(config/absorb), .2(hooks/ move + symlink), .3(skills/extensions/registry.json). Phase P1=purge plugin: .4(CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT purge), .5(hooks.json+settings.json local paths). Phase P2=installer: .6(claude-runtime-sync.ts rewrite), .7(xt install drift detect). Phase P3=cleanup: .8(rm prepack/postpack), .9(rm plugins/+.claude-plugin/). Tests: .10(unit registry), .11(shell hooks), .12(E2E install — gates .9). Start with xtrm-gl8b.1.

main-guard.cjs calls process.exit() directly so it cannot be require()d in tests. Tests must use spawnSync to invoke it as a subprocess. Use MAIN_GUARD_PROTECTED_BRANCHES env var to control which branch is treated as protected in tests.
Extracted skills symlink helpers into cli/src/core/skills-scaffold.ts; both init and install now call shared ensureAgentsSkillsSymlink to keep .agents/skills and .claude/skills wiring consistent.
memory-processor now prioritizes existing memory.md and last 3 report sections before bd recall; registry scaffold now hard-skips user-owned memory.md and skills/user paths even with --force

Pi does NOT auto-discover packages from node_modules — settings.json packages entry is mandatory. Use 'pi install npm:<pkg>' which handles both npm install AND settings.json creation. Local installs go to .pi/npm/node_modules/. Model after pi-gitnexus: package.json needs keywords:['pi-package'], pi:{extensions:['./src/index.ts']}. Pi loads TS via jiti — no build needed.

Pi extension anti-patterns confirmed in PR #91 review: (1) skill_activator.py must only run in before_agent_start — adding it to tool_result is a regression that fires on every tool call.
